evidence: Multiple packages define parallel seam-runtime structs with function fields to inject dependencies instead of sharing a common abstraction layer (for example, `goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go` and `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go`).

`DockerListener` and `DockerSubscriptionHandler` in `goseg/routines/docker.go` run unbounded select loops in startup goroutines without coordinated shutdown control, which couples lifecycle management to package-level invocation paths.
Importer archive extraction paths in `goseg/importer/importer.go` and `goseg/importer/extract.go` split by extension but repeat extraction/monitoring logic instead of a strategy abstraction.
auth/auth.go builds a token map with duplicate `"token"` literal keys, which is a structural code-quality defect and a sign of fragile auth payload construction.
`goseg/leak/handler.go` parses protocol data with direct slicing (`result[5:]`) and assumes a fixed framing length without guard checks.
`goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go` schedules a poll loop in `DockerPoller` but exits on first tick, so periodic work is never sustained.
`goseg/docker/orchestration/minio.go` couples MinIO service-account creation to substring matching of CLI output (`"successfully"`), which is brittle and can block idempotent setup.

Multiple orchestration/lifecycle paths discard errors from container-status/lookup calls and continue as if no container exists, which changes outcome from explicit failure to silent recovery attempts.
In leak broadcast state update, serialization failures are logged but treated as a successful no-error return, so callers cannot distinguish successful state mutation from partial failure.

findings.summary: Parallel runtime seam abstractions are redefined in multiple packages, creating maintenance drift and inconsistent dependency policy.
evidence: `routines/runtime_seams.go` and `docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` both define base/runtime structs with duplicated function-pointer dependency members and constructor wiring over the same configuration-backed sources.
`routines/docker.go` and orchestration modules each rely on these duplicated seams independently, so a policy or dependency change must be replicated across files instead of applied centrally.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared seam abstraction layer (for example a small `runtime_dependencies` package) with reusable builder helpers and a minimal cross-package interface for container orchestration/config/logging/test seams; wire package-specific fields as composed overlays instead of duplicating full runtime scaffolding.

findings.summary: Docker event listener/subscriber routines have no explicit stop contract, so background lifecycle control is pushed into global goroutine orchestration rather than the abstraction interface.
evidence: `DockerListener` creates `ctx := context.Background()` and calls `cli.Events(ctx, ...)` while `main.go` launches it as a fire-and-forget goroutine (`go routines.DockerListener()`) with no cancellable handle.
The function loops forever on `select { case event := <-messages; case err := <-errs }` with no `ctx.Done()` or close-channel handling, so callers cannot express graceful stop/restart boundaries.
Other background subsystems in the same code path (for example `CheckVersionLoop` and `startServer` flow) are also goroutine-spawned without shared lifecycle wiring, increasing subsystem coupling.
findings.suggestion: Refactor `DockerListener` to accept a runtime context and/or stop channel in its constructor/runtime struct; have `main` own a cancellation scope and close it on shutdown paths so tests and orchestrators can deterministically stop/restart listener loops.

findings.summary: Archive extraction paths are split by extension with near-duplicate control logic rather than a shared extraction strategy abstraction.
evidence: `extractUploadedPier` switches on extensions and delegates to separate extractor functions, then each extractor performs similar event-loop/progress behavior inline.

`extractZip`, `extractTarGz`, and `extractTar` each manually reimplement timeout setup, directory creation, entry filtering, output writes, and progress publishing details with slight behavioral drift.
Progress semantics differ between implementations (`fileCount`/size weighting differs per extractor), making extension handlers harder to reason about as independent policy changes evolve.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a common extraction strategy interface with shared archive traversal helpers and a single progress publisher; keep format-specific handlers focused only on parsing/writer-specific reader behavior to reduce duplicated abstraction depth.

findings.summary: Auth token payload map declares duplicate `token` key, risking invalid compile/contract behavior.
evidence: `CreateToken` sets `"token"` twice in the same map literal, making the entry definition ambiguous and likely a real compile-time defect in Go map construction.
Import path consumers in `importer` expect a single stable token mapping (`Token.ID`, `Token.Token`) and the duplicate obscures intended token output shape.
findings.suggestion: Remove the duplicate key and keep a single canonical `Token` entry; if additional encoded fields are needed, use distinct map keys or a typed payload struct and validate consumers in importer accordingly.

findings.summary: Leak websocket action parser panics on short or malformed packets due unguarded slicing.
evidence: `handleAction` calls `stripped := result[5:]` before validating `len(result)`.
Malformed inputs (malicious or partial network payloads) can cause out-of-range panics and tear down bug-reporting/handler execution paths.
No defensive protocol-length check exists before `reverseLittleEndian`/decode steps.
findings.suggestion: Add explicit length/frame validation in `handleAction` (e.g., minimum bytes + optional checksum/type checks) and return/log malformed payload errors rather than slicing blindly.

findings.summary: Docker polling loop returns after first tick, so recurring lifecycle work never runs.
evidence: `DockerPoller` uses `for { select { case <-ticker.C: ... return } }` which exits on the first tick.
Name and implementation imply a long-lived poller, but current control flow makes it effectively one-shot.
findings.suggestion: Replace `return` with periodic dispatch (or remove `select` entirely and run the poll handler directly in the loop) so the function continuously polls and calls container orchestration reconciliation work.

findings.summary: MinIO account bootstrap is coupled to CLI response text and fails when expected phrasing differs.
evidence: `setMinIOAdminAccountWithRuntime` removes an existing service account and only proceeds to add a new one if command output contains `"successfully"`.
`createMinIOServiceAccount` callers (e.g., `toggleMinIOLink`) interpret this as fatal and cannot enable integration when removal output format differs or no prior account exists.
findings.suggestion: Parse exit codes from `mc` commands directly and make creation idempotent: treat "not found" as non-fatal, attempt add after remove (or create directly) and only fail on explicit command errors.

findings.summary: Decision points ignore container lookup errors and proceed as if conditions are healthy.
evidence: `ensureRunningContainer` uses `existingContainer, _ := FindContainer(plan.Name)` and treats any non-nil error as `nil` state in the switch, skipping the failure signal and potentially creating/starting containers via blind recovery.

`cleanupMinIOContainerForStart` also uses `existingContainer, _ := FindContainer(containerName)` and proceeds only on pointer nil, so lookup failures can hide and cause unnecessary delete/start attempts.
`setMinIOAdminAccountWithRuntime` polls `status, _ := rt.getContainerRunningStatus("mc")` and ignores errors, so transient Docker API failures can turn into indefinite waiting loops.
findings.suggestion: Capture and handle these lookup errors explicitly: return/publish a failure path when status/container queries fail, and only retry on explicit transient classes with bounded backoff. In polling loops, add timeout/circuit-breaker and fail with a surfaced operational error instead of looping forever.

findings.summary: Broadcast update returns success when payload marshal fails, masking mutation failure.
evidence: `updateBroadcast` logs `json.Marshal(newBroadcast)` errors and returns `oldBroadcast, nil` rather than propagating an error.
`updateBroadcast` also checks per-recipient `wrapperUrbitBytes, err := json.Marshal(wrappedUrbit)` and only sends on nil, but never returns/report this failure to callers.
Tests in `goseg/leak/broadcast_test.go` validate `updateBroadcast` error behavior but do not observe partial-failure telemetry when marshalling fails, so this silent path is unobservable to callers.
findings.suggestion: Return a wrapped error when either marshal fails, and keep the function output explicit about failed updates (e.g., return unchanged state + error). Also propagate/aggregate per-recipient marshal failures so broadcast callers can react or alert on partial fanout failure.
